steps.description: Click the "Declarative Agent" option in the "New Project" dropdown within the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit extension in Visual Studio Code.

Click the "Add a Copilot connector" dropdown option in the "Create Declarative Agent" dialog within the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface. The red crosshair highlights the exact selection in the list, making the action precise.
Click the "Default folder (/home/vscode/AgentsToolkitProjects)" option in the "Workspace Folder" selection dropdown at the top of the Visual Studio Code window.
Type  ${{var:app_name:vscuse_app_#####}}  into the "Input an application name" text field in the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit extension popup within Visual Studio Code.
Press Enter to confirm the application name ${{var:app_name:vscuse_app_#####}} in the input dialog at the top of the Visual Studio Code interface.
Click the text input field in the "Application Name" dialog at the top of the Visual Studio Code window to set or edit the application's name.
Press the Enter key to confirm the application name input ("vscuse_app_vRJau") in the "Input an application name" text field within the command palette of Visual Studio Code.
Type 'testconnectorname' into the "Add a connector name" input field in the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit extension panel within Visual Studio Code.
Press the Enter key to confirm the connector name "testconnectorname" in the "Add a connector name" input dialog within the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit extension in Visual Studio Code.
Type 'testconnectorid' into the "Add a connection ID" input box at the top of the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface in Visual Studio Code.
Press the Enter key to confirm the input "testconnectorid" in the "Add a connection ID" dialog within the Microsoft 365 Agents Toolkit interface in Visual Studio Code.
@assertion there's "Preview README.md" tab header in the Visual Studio Code and 'Copilot connector Template' is exists
Click the "Preview README.md" tab in the Visual Studio Code editor to activate the README file preview pane.

parameters.sample:
=== Generated Script ===
Language: bash

```bash
env_file="/home/vscode/AgentsToolkitProjects/vscuse_app_h43Wp/env/.env.dev"

if [ ! -f "$env_file" ]; then
  echo ".env.dev file not found!"
  exit 1
fi

tmpfile=$(mktemp)

awk '
  /^\s*CONNECTOR_REPOS\s*=/{print "CONNECTOR_REPOS='microsoft/Agent365-Samples'"; next}
  /^\s*CONNECTOR_ID\s*=/{print "CONNECTOR_ID='testconnectorid'"; next}
  {print}
' "$env_file" > "$tmpfile"

mv "$tmpfile" "$env_file"

cat "$env_file"
```

=== Execution Results ===
Exit Code: 0

=== STDOUT ===
# This file includes environment variables that will be committed to git by default.

# Built-in environment variables
TEAMSFX_ENV=dev
APP_NAME_SUFFIX=dev
CONNECTOR_ID=testconnectorid
CONNECTOR_NAME=testconnectorname
CONNECTOR_DESCRIPTION=Connection that indexes all GitHub issues from a specific owner and repo.
CONNECTOR_REPOS=microsoft/Agent365-Samples

# Updating Azure resources
AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID=
AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP_NAME=
RESOURCE_SUFFIX=

# Generated during provision, you can also add your own variables.

✓ Script executed successfully

steps.description: @code fill into /home/vscode/AgentsToolkitProjects/${{var:app_name}}/env/.env.local, replace CONNECTOR_REPOS= 'microsoft/Agent365-Samples' and save the file.
Click the "Explorer" icon in the Visual Studio Code sidebar to open the project file explorer.
Click the "env" folder in the Explorer panel of the Visual Studio Code application to select it.
Click the ".env.local" file in the "env" folder within the Explorer sidebar of Visual Studio Code, highlighting it for selection or opening.
Click the ".env.local" tab in the VS Code editor to focus on the environment variable configuration file within the VSCUSE_APP_JRHC9 project.

Click the 'Explorer' icon in the Visual Studio Code sidebar to open the project file tree. The interaction occurs in the VS Code workspace interface, specifically on the left sidebar where the file explorer is located, indicated by a red crosshair over the folder icon.
Click the "Debug" dropdown button in the Run and Debug panel of the Visual Studio Code application interface, as indicated by the red crosshair at coordinates (142, 55).
